title: Why Movement Matters More Than Meditation for Some Survivors
researcherTopic: sensorimotor psychotherapy, the body in trauma treatment
metaDescription: For trauma survivors who find meditation panic-inducing, somatic movement offers a powerful alternative. Learn why movement is key to healing the nervous system.
imageDescription: A pair of bare feet are firmly planted on a wooden floor, bathed in the warm, gentle light of early morning. The scene evokes a sense of grounding, stability, and the quiet strength of returning to one's body.
htmlContent:
I've sat with hundreds of people who carry a particular kind of shame—the shame of being unable to meditate. They describe closing their eyes and feeling not peace, but a rising tide of panic, a jolt of electricity in their limbs, an unshakeable conviction that if they stay still for one more second, the world will end. For years, they've been told that stillness is the answer, that the path to healing is paved with mindfulness apps and silent retreats, and their failure to find solace there feels like yet another personal failing in a life already defined by the weight of trauma. But what if the problem isn't the person, but the prescription? What if, for a nervous system wired for threat, the demand for absolute stillness is not a pathway to peace but a reenactment of the very powerlessness that caused the wound in the first place? The quiet hum of a meditation hall can feel indistinguishable from the tense silence of a childhood home where danger lurked around every corner. The instruction to simply “watch the breath” can feel like an impossible task when the breath itself is held hostage by a lifetime of bracing for impact. For these survivors, movement isn't a distraction from the present moment; it is the only viable way to enter it.

The Tyranny of Stillness for the Traumatized Body

For a nervous system shaped by complex trauma, stillness is often interpreted as a threat. The state of hypervigilance that defines C-PTSD is not a cognitive choice; it is a physiological reality, a state in which the body’s alarm systems are perpetually scanning for danger. When we are forced into stillness—whether in a meditation posture or simply by trying to fall asleep—the external distractions that keep the background hum of anxiety at bay fall away. The body, left without a task, begins to speak in its native language: sensation. And for the survivor, that language is often one of terror, of constriction, of un-discharged fight-or-flight energy that has been locked in the tissues for decades. This isn't a failure of discipline. It is the predictable response of a system that learned, correctly, that immobility was dangerous. Whether it was the freeze response during an overwhelming event or the forced stillness required to avoid provoking an unstable caregiver, the body remembers. It remembers that not moving, not acting, not escaping, was a precursor to pain. To then ask that same body to sit in silent stillness and “be with what is” can feel like a cruel joke. It’s a demand to tolerate the very state that it has spent a lifetime trying to avoid. In my years working in this territory, I've seen countless individuals believe they are “bad at meditation” when, in fact, their bodies are simply doing their job, screaming for the movement that equals survival.
Most of what passes for healing is just rearranging the furniture in a burning house.
We are told to quiet the mind, but for many, the mind is the only place that feels remotely manageable. The body is a landscape of unprocessed grief, rage, and terror. To drop into it without a map, without a resource, is to invite overwhelm. The impulse to fidget, to pace, to stretch, to simply get up and walk away from the meditation cushion is not a sign of spiritual failure. It is a sign of profound somatic intelligence. It is the body’s wisdom, crying out for a different path, one that doesn't begin with stillness, but earns it. We must first learn to speak the body's language, and that language, more often than not, begins with movement. It's a truth that stands in stark contrast to so much of the wellness industry's advice, but it's a truth the nervous system knows intimately. True healing doesn't start by forcing a state of calm; it starts by honoring the state that is actually present, even if that state is one of profound activation. From there, a new possibility can emerge, one that is not imposed from the outside but discovered from within.

Movement as a Dialogue with the Nervous System

What if we reframed movement not as exercise or as a distraction, but as a direct and sophisticated form of communication with the deepest parts of ourselves? Every gesture, every stretch, every step can be an opportunity to engage with the body’s stored history, not to erase it, but to update it. When a survivor of complex trauma engages in conscious movement—whether it's a gentle yoga flow, a walk in the woods, or simply swaying to music—they are offering their nervous system a new experience. They are demonstrating, in real time, that it is possible to feel activation and then come back to a state of relative ease. This is the essence of pendulation, a core concept in somatic therapies: moving between a state of charge and a state of discharge, gently and intentionally. This process is not about catharsis in the way we often think of it—not a dramatic explosion of emotion, but a slow, titrated unwinding. It might look like noticing an impulse to clench the fists and, instead of suppressing it, allowing the clench to happen fully and then consciously, slowly, releasing it. It might be feeling a wave of anxiety and choosing to walk, feeling the feet on the ground, letting the rhythm of the steps become an anchor. This is not about “getting rid of” the feeling, but about giving it space to move *through* the body instead of getting stuck *in* it. A client once described this as the difference between being tossed in an ocean of panic and learning to surf the waves. The waves are the same, but the relationship to them is fundamentally different. This dialogue is profoundly reparative. Trauma teaches the body that its impulses are dangerous and that its sensations are unbearable. Conscious movement teaches the opposite. It says: *Your impulse to move is valid. Your sensations are not a threat; they are information. You can feel this, and you will not be destroyed.* It is a practice of reclaiming agency over one’s own physical experience. For someone whose body was once a site of helplessness, this is nothing short of revolutionary. It is a way of saying to the oldest parts of the brain, the parts that don't understand language but understand sensation perfectly, that the war is over. The threat has passed. You are here, now, and you are safe enough to move.
You cannot think your way into a felt sense of safety. The body has its own logic.
This is why practices like somatic experiencing and sensorimotor psychotherapy are so effective. They bypass the storytelling mind and engage directly with the body’s narrative. They recognize that the procedural memories stored in the nervous system—the postures, the gestures, the patterns of tension—are where the trauma truly lives. By bringing gentle, curious awareness to these patterns and introducing new movement possibilities, we are, in a very real sense, rewriting the past from the inside out. We are creating a new set of memories, memories of agency, of resource, and of completion.

Pat Ogden and the Language of the Body

No one has articulated the importance of this bodily dialogue more clearly than Pat Ogden, the founder of Sensorimotor Psychotherapy. Ogden’s work is built on a simple but profound premise: the body, just as much as the mind, is a source of intelligence and a vehicle for healing. She recognized that for trauma survivors, the cognitive and emotional elements of their experience were often disconnected from the physical sensations and motor patterns that held the trauma in place. A person could talk about a traumatic event for years, understanding it intellectually, but still find themselves trapped in the same cycles of anxiety, dissociation, and physical pain because the body’s story had never been heard. Ogden’s genius was in creating a methodology that systematically integrates the body into the therapeutic process. She teaches therapists and clients to track “the five languages of the body”: sensation, movement, gesture, posture, and emotion. Instead of focusing solely on the narrative of what happened, the focus shifts to how the experience is being organized in the present moment, in the physical self. A client might be talking about feeling trapped, and Ogden would gently guide them to notice what is happening in their body as they speak that word. Is there a clenching in the jaw? A shallowness in the breath? A tension in the shoulders as if preparing to fight? This is not just about mindfulness; it is about intervention. Once a pattern is identified—for example, the shoulders hunching forward in a protective posture—the next step is to explore a new possibility. What would it be like to, just for a moment, roll the shoulders back? Not as a command to “have better posture,” but as an experiment. What happens in the body when this new shape is made? Does the breath change? Does a new feeling emerge? This is what Ogden calls a “somatic resource.” It is a physical action that helps the nervous system regulate itself. For many survivors, discovering these resources is like learning a new alphabet, one that allows them to finally write a different ending to their story. It’s a process that honors the idea that healing the fragmented self requires more than just insight. In my own work, I’ve seen the power of this approach transform people’s lives. I recall a client who had spent years in talk therapy trying to overcome a deep-seated feeling of worthlessness. It was only when we began to work with the subtle, habitual collapse in her chest—a posture she had adopted in childhood to make herself smaller and less of a target—that something truly shifted. By practicing the simple movement of gently lifting her sternum and breathing into her heart space, she was, for the first time, embodying a sense of dignity. She didn't have to think her way into feeling worthy; she was accessing the feeling directly, through the language of her own body. This is the profound gift of Ogden’s work: it reminds us that the body is not just a container for our pain, but a wise and powerful ally in our liberation.

Beyond Catharsis: The Wisdom of Somatic Unwinding

Our culture has a deep fascination with catharsis—the big, dramatic release of emotion. We imagine that healing from trauma must involve screaming, crying, and punching pillows. And while there can be a place for such expressions, the somatic approach to healing offers a more sustainable and often more profound alternative: the slow, titrated unwinding of the nervous system. This is not about avoiding intense feelings, but about building the capacity to be with them without becoming overwhelmed. It is the difference between a dam breaking and a river finding its natural course. Somatic unwinding is a process of listening. It begins with bringing a gentle, non-judgmental awareness to the body’s moment-to-moment experience. You might notice a knot of tension in your stomach. The instinct, conditioned by a lifetime of avoidance, is to either ignore it or to immediately try to “fix” it—to breathe into it, to stretch it, to make it go away. The practice of unwinding invites a different response: to simply be with it. To notice its size, its shape, its temperature, its texture. To become curious about it. What is this sensation? What is its quality? Without a story, without a judgment, what is actually here?
When you stop trying to fix the moment, something remarkable happens — the moment becomes workable.
From this place of gentle observation, movement can emerge not as a command, but as an organic impulse. As you stay with the knot in your stomach, you might feel a subtle urge to curl forward, to bring your knees toward your chest. The practice is to follow this impulse, slowly and mindfully. As you curl, you are not just “doing a stretch”; you are honoring a protective response that has likely been held in your body for a very long time. You are allowing the story of that tension to be told, not in words, but in movement. And then, after staying with the curl for a few breaths, you might feel a counter-impulse: a gentle urge to open, to stretch, to unfurl. This is the unwinding. It is the body’s natural, intelligent way of completing a response that was interrupted long ago. This process is deeply empowering. It teaches the nervous system that it can move through states of contraction and into states of expansion. It builds what is known as “affect tolerance”—the ability to experience a wide range of emotions and sensations without shutting down or blowing up. It is a gradual process of reclaiming the territory of the body, one sensation at a time. It’s a journey that requires patience and a willingness to let go of the idea that healing should be a linear or dramatic process. It is a quiet revolution, one that happens in the subtle shifts of breath and the small, mindful movements that, over time, restore a sense of wholeness and trust in the body’s innate wisdom. It is a path that understands that the architecture of returning is built not on grand gestures, but on the foundation of small, consistent acts of presence.

Re-inhabiting the Self, One Gesture at a Time

For many who have endured complex trauma, the body does not feel like a home. It feels more like a haunted house, a place of unpredictable dangers and painful memories. Dissociation, in its many forms, becomes a necessary survival strategy—a way to disconnect from the overwhelming sensations of a body in distress. The journey of healing, then, is a process of re-inhabitation, of slowly, carefully, and intentionally moving back into the physical self. This is not a single event, but a practice, one that unfolds one gesture, one breath, one moment at a time. This process often begins at the periphery. For someone who is highly dissociated, the idea of feeling the core of their body can be terrifying. So, we start with the hands and feet. Can you feel your feet on the floor? Can you press them into the ground and feel the ground press back? This simple act of proprioception—the sense of the body’s position in space—can be a powerful anchor to the present moment. It is a way of saying, *I am here. I am on solid ground.* These are not just mindless exercises; they are acts of somatic engagement. They are small, manageable ways of saying hello to a body that has been ignored for a very long time. As a sense of resource builds, we can begin to move inward. The practice might involve placing a hand on the heart and simply noticing the rhythm of the heartbeat, or placing a hand on the belly and feeling the gentle rise and fall of the breath. For many, this is the first time they have ever consciously and kindly touched their own body. It is a gesture of profound self-compassion, a way of offering the comfort and safety that was not available in the past. It is a practice that recognizes that understanding your internal family of parts often begins with tending to the physical home they all share. Over time, this practice of re-inhabitation becomes more fluid and integrated into daily life. It might look like noticing the tension in your shoulders while driving and consciously letting them drop. It might be feeling a surge of anger and choosing to take a brisk walk instead of lashing out. It is a gradual process of becoming an embodied being, someone who lives not just from the neck up, but in the full, vibrant reality of their physical self. It is a journey from alienation to intimacy, from fragmentation to wholeness. It is the slow, patient work of turning a haunted house back into a home, a place of safety, of refuge, and of belonging.
What we call 'the present moment' is not a place you go. It's the only place you've ever been.

When Stillness Becomes a Resource, Not a Threat

There is a beautiful paradox in this work: by embracing movement, we create the conditions for stillness to finally become safe. The stillness that is earned through somatic exploration is entirely different from the stillness that is imposed. The former is a choice, a resource, a state of deep and vibrant presence. The latter is a command, a threat, a state of frozen terror. The journey from one to the other is the essence of healing from complex trauma. When we have given the body the movement it craves, when we have allowed it to discharge stored energy, to complete interrupted responses, and to find new somatic resources, the nervous system begins to settle. The background hum of hypervigilance quiets down. The constant scanning for threat is replaced by a sense of groundedness and ease. From this place, stillness is no longer a trigger. It is a relief. It is the feeling of coming home after a long journey. Closing the eyes is no longer an invitation to panic, but an opportunity to rest in the quiet landscape of a body that has finally found a measure of peace. This earned stillness is not empty. It is full. It is full of sensation—the gentle rhythm of the breath, the warmth of the skin, the subtle tingling of aliveness. Because we have practiced being with these sensations in a safe and titrated way, they are no longer overwhelming. They are simply the texture of being alive. This is the state that the meditation teachers were talking about all along, but it is a state that cannot be accessed through force of will. It must be arrived at through the body. It is a destination that can only be reached by taking the scenic route, the one that winds through the messy, beautiful, and wise territory of our own physical experience. This is the ultimate gift of a movement-based approach to trauma healing. It does not reject stillness, but honors the path that must be walked to arrive there. It recognizes that for the survivor, the body must first be convinced, through experience, that it is safe to stop running. Only then can it truly rest. Only then can we discover the profound truth that lies at the heart of all contemplative practice: that beneath the turbulence of our thoughts and the storms of our emotions, there is a place of deep, unshakable quiet that has been here all along, waiting patiently for us to come home.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does traditional meditation make my anxiety worse?

For a nervous system wired by trauma for hypervigilance, the command to be still can feel like a reenactment of the immobility and helplessness experienced during past overwhelming events. This can trigger the body's alarm system, increasing anxiety, panic, and dissociation rather than reducing it. Your body is not failing at meditation; it is successfully identifying a perceived threat based on its deeply learned history.

What are some simple movements I can start with?

Begin with gentle, grounding movements that anchor you in the present. Try slowly pressing your feet into the floor and noticing the sensation of the ground supporting you. You could also try gently pushing against a wall to feel your own strength, or simply swaying from side to side as if you were a tree in the wind. The key is to move slowly and with curiosity, paying attention to the physical sensations without judgment.

Is the goal to eventually be able to sit still in meditation?

The goal is not to force yourself into a specific posture, but to cultivate a nervous system that is flexible and resilient enough to choose stillness when it feels right. For many, the path involves embracing movement to build capacity and discharge stored stress. Over time, as the body learns it is safe, periods of stillness may naturally become more accessible and even desirable, but it becomes a resource you can choose rather than a rule you must follow.

The journey of re-inhabiting the body is not about achieving a perfect state of calm, but about learning to be a compassionate companion to yourself, wherever you are. It is the tender, moment-by-moment practice of returning to the only home you have ever truly had.
